content.assessment: Reciprocal contracts presume a level of municipal fiscal and demographic stability that Hungary's most affected villages lack. The proposal's viability depends heavily on which municipalities can credibly commit to network reorganization; those already below the 10% under-18 threshold have little to offer in reciprocal bargaining. This lens finds the mechanism plausible for moderately declining but not yet collapsed cohorts, with limited applicability to the most demographically extreme third of settlements.
opportunities: Age-structure divergence data (91 young vs. 42 old municipalities) could inform differentiated contract terms by demographic cluster rather than a uniform national template
strengths: Time-bound contracts (three-year cycles) align reasonably with medium-term planning horizons for gradually declining rather than collapsed cohorts
Could differentiate treatment by regional variation, addressing e.g. Zala's steeper 25% birth decline distinctly from Budapest's 22%
threats: Municipalities in the most demographically depleted areas (Vas, Zala, average age above 50) may be structurally unable to fulfill reciprocal commitments, producing asymmetric contracts that function as delayed closure rather than genuine co-responsibility
Continuing TFR decline (1.31, still falling) shortens the effective life of any three-year contract before renegotiation is needed under a worse baseline
weaknesses: Contract logic assumes municipal capacity to negotiate and commit resources, which is absent in the third of settlements under 500 inhabitants with minimal fiscal base
No mechanism specified for settlements that have already reached the zero-enrollment cliff, where there is no cohort left to bargain over
